name: run_scene_edit_script
description_lines: Run a one-off scene edit worker script against exactly one target scene. Use this when the request is procedural, repetitive, or easier to express as native Godot code than manual scene patching.

Mental model: this tool gives you a real instantiated scene object graph, but NOT a fully running gameplay scene like pressing Run Current Scene. You can traverse nodes, read and write properties, mutate resources, add/remove children, and save; but many SceneTree-dependent APIs behave differently.

Name caveat: `run_scene_edit_script` is meant to make the scene-editing mental model clearer. Treat it as a scene/resource mutation worker, not as a generic live editor session or full runtime game launch.

Power-user inspection model: because the worker is an editor-side `@tool` script, you can use it to inspect many Godot/editor/project things when normal inspection tools are not enough, including ClassDB runtime behavior, native addon/GDExtension classes, ResourceLoader/ResourceSaver flows, standalone `.tres` resources, and project resources. Use this power carefully: keep probes tiny, targeted, and bounded; log concise findings with `ctx.log(...)`; and do not brute-force scan a whole project or run editor-unsafe code.

Tool choice rule: use `get_class_info` first when you only need class API docs, methods, properties, inheritance, enums, or signals. Use `run_scene_edit_script` for class/resource inspection only when you need to execute real editor-side Godot code, such as instantiating an addon class, reading runtime-only property lists, saving a resource through `ResourceSaver`, or probing APIs that static class docs cannot answer.

Scene-as-context rule: for editor-side probes that do not actually need the target scene, pass a small safe existing scene as the context scene. Do not call `ctx.mark_modified()` or scene-mutating helpers unless you intentionally want to save that scene. In this mode `modified=false` and `scene_saved=false` are expected and do not mean the probe failed.

Scope limit: this tool is for scene nodes, scene-owned or scene-assigned Godot resources, and targeted standalone Godot resources loaded/created through Godot APIs. Do NOT use it to directly edit imported source assets or import-pipeline outputs such as `.png`, `.jpg`, `.webp`, `.wav`, `.ogg`, `.mp3`, `.glb`, `.gltf`, `.fbx`, `.ttf`, or `.otf` and expect those source/imported files themselves to be rewritten.

Imported-asset rule: it is fine to assign imported assets to nodes, wrap them with authored resources, or create local scene overrides around them. It is NOT the right tool for changing the underlying imported asset file or its import settings; use the appropriate file-editing or import workflow for that.

Before using this tool for scene or built-in resource edits, use `get_class_info` first to inspect the target class API, including its properties, methods, enums/constants, tutorials, runtime parent chain, and direct children.

The tool accepts either raw `code` or an existing `script_path`. If `code` is provided, it is saved to a temp `.gd` under `res://.fennara/tmp/editor_scripts/`; every response includes the effective `script_path` pointing at that saved file (or the path you passed in).

**CRITICAL — failures and large scripts:** If the worker script was long or the run failed for any reason (parse/type errors from `script_diagnostics`, runtime errors in `runtime_errors`, engine errors triggered by bad logging, wrong output, etc.), **do not discard the temp file and paste a brand-new full `code` argument.** Treat the returned `script_path` as the source of truth: use **`write_or_update_file`** to patch that exact file in small steps, then rerun with **`scene_path` + `script_path`** only. Repeat patch → rerun until diagnostics and runtime are clean.

**Default retry workflow:** (1) Note `script_path` from the tool result. (2) `write_or_update_file` on that path to fix only what broke. (3) Rerun `run_scene_edit_script` with `scene_path` + `script_path` only. Use fresh inline `code` mainly for the first creation before any temp path exists.

The script must be `@tool`, extend `RefCounted`, and define `func run(ctx) -> void`. It is NOT attached to the scene. It runs once against the target scene in memory. Existing scenes are only saved back to `scene_path` when the script actually marks the context modified; read-only inspection runs return logs without rewriting the scene.

Inherited-scene preservation: if the target scene has an inherited root such as `[node name="SomeScene" instance=ExtResource(...)]`, the tool loads it in inherited edit mode and attempts to save only valid overrides/additions while preserving the inherited root. Do not try to manually rewrite the `.tscn` root from text to preserve inheritance; the tool is designed to guard this case.

Inherited-scene safety failure: if Godot would flatten an inherited root while saving, the tool refuses to keep that save, restores the original scene file, returns `success=false`, `scene_saved=false`, `inherited_root_scene=true`, and an error explaining that preserving the inherited root failed. When this happens, do not assume your scene mutation applied. Patch the returned `script_path` or choose a narrower edit, then rerun.

Inherited-scene editing rule: for inherited scenes, make narrow property/resource overrides on existing nodes whenever possible. Avoid replacing the root, clearing broad inherited subtrees, recursively owning inherited children, or rebuilding inherited structure. Those operations can force flattening or be rejected by the tool's inherited-root guard.

For existing scenes, `ctx.get_scene_root()` returns the loaded root and the script must modify it. For new scenes, `ctx.get_scene_root()` starts null and the script must create a root node and call `ctx.set_scene_root(root)` exactly once. Call `ctx.own(node)` on newly created raw nodes/subtrees so they are serialized. For PackedScene instances, use `ctx.instance_scene(parent, scene_path, desired_name)` instead of manual `PackedScene.instantiate()` + `ctx.own(...)`.

New-scene ordering rule: for brand-new scenes, call `ctx.set_scene_root(root)` before the first `ctx.own(child)`. `ctx.own()` requires an active scene root. Do not call `ctx.set_scene_root(root)` again at the end; it is a one-time registration, not a save/finalize step.

Available `ctx` API: `ctx.get_scene_root() -> Node`, `ctx.get_scene_path() -> String`, `ctx.get_scene_exists() -> bool`, `ctx.set_scene_root(root: Node)`, `ctx.own(node: Node)`, `ctx.instance_scene(parent: Node, scene_path: String, desired_name: String) -> Node`, `ctx.log(message: String)`, `ctx.error(message: String)`, `ctx.get_node_or_null(path: String) -> Node`, `ctx.find_nodes_by_name(name: String) -> Array[Node]`, `ctx.ensure_unique_child_name(parent: Node, desired_name: String) -> String`, `ctx.remove_node(path: String) -> bool`, `ctx.clear_children(node: Node)`, and `ctx.mark_modified()`.

Important rules: use `ctx.get_scene_root()` for existing scenes; call `ctx.set_scene_root(root)` only for brand new scenes; call `ctx.own(node)` after adding each raw newly created node or subtree you want saved; use `ctx.instance_scene(parent, "res://path/to/scene.tscn", "DesiredName")` for instanced scenes; use `ctx.log(...)` for structured logs returned to the model; use `ctx.error(...)` to stop with a clear runtime error instead of silently failing.

PackedScene ownership rule: `ctx.own()` is only for raw nodes created with `Node.new()`. Do not call `ctx.own()` on a PackedScene instance root or on any child inside that instance. The tool now refuses both cases because owning instance internals can flatten/duplicate the child nodes in the parent scene. `ctx.instance_scene(...)` owns only the instance root, preserving the subscene instance correctly in the saved `.tscn`.

Node naming rule: always assign explicit meaningful `name` values to nodes you create unless there is a very strong reason not to. Godot auto-generates names like `@Label@21109`, `@HBoxContainer@21112`, or `@ProgressBar@21110` when a node has no explicit name; these are discouraged because they make scenes harder to inspect, diff, script, animate, and maintain. Name important, visible, repeated, scripted, animated, inspected, or maintained nodes immediately, e.g. `StrengthRow`, `StrengthLabel`, `StrengthBar`, `StrengthValue`, `DexterityRow`, `DexterityBar`. Purely decorative generated children may use auto names only with a strong reason.

Danger pattern: a broad recursive ownership helper can still be wrong even if it skips the instance root. Bad: `func own_recursive(ctx, node): if String(node.scene_file_path) == "": ctx.own(node); for child in node.get_children(): own_recursive(ctx, child)` followed by `var card = ctx.instance_scene(grid, "res://item_card.tscn", "Card"); own_recursive(ctx, root)`. That skips `Card` but then walks into `Card/Main` and tries to own component internals.

Safe recursive pattern: only recurse into raw children and stop at scene instance boundaries. Example: `func own_raw_subtree(ctx, node): ctx.own(node); for child in node.get_children(): if String(child.scene_file_path) != "": ctx.log("Skipped PackedScene instance %s from %s" % [child.name, child.scene_file_path]); continue; own_raw_subtree(ctx, child)`. Use this only for raw authored subtrees; use `ctx.instance_scene(...)` for reusable/component scenes.

Info-extraction rule: `run_scene_edit_script` is also valid for read-heavy tasks where you want structured information out of scene-owned resources without inventing a separate tool. For tasks like listing animations, summarizing tracks, reporting keyframe counts, inspecting group membership, or printing resource details, prefer collecting the data and emitting it through `ctx.log(...)` so the result comes back in `logs`.

Inspection/query rule: when using `run_scene_edit_script` only to inspect scene state, the script MUST be side-effect free. Read data and emit concise `ctx.log(...)` lines, but do NOT call `ctx.mark_modified()` and do NOT call mutating ctx helpers such as `ctx.own(...)`, `ctx.instance_scene(...)`, `ctx.remove_node(...)`, or `ctx.clear_children(...)`. If the script leaves the context unmodified, the tool reports `modified=false` and `scene_saved=false`.

Timeout/performance rule: MCP clients may be configured to wait several minutes for Fennara tool results, but this tool must still be written as a bounded, targeted worker. Do not use the longer timeout as permission for brute-force full-scene scans.

Large-scene rule: before scanning a large root/world scene, prefer `get_scene_tree`, `get_node_properties`, file search, or a tiny probe to narrow the target. If a full traversal is truly necessary, log concise progress checkpoints with `ctx.log(...)`, skip irrelevant subtrees early, cache expensive calculations, cap sample output, and avoid recomputing category/parent/AABB/resource data in multiple loops.

Retry-after-timeout rule: if this tool times out or becomes slow on a large scene, do not rerun the same broad inline `code`. Use the returned/saved `script_path` when available, patch it to narrow the scan or add checkpoints, then rerun with `scene_path` + `script_path`.

Typing rule: use explicit type annotations for all meaningful local variables in worker scripts, including primitives (`float`, `int`, `bool`, `String`), nodes, resources, arrays, dictionaries, and values returned from `ctx`, `get(...)`, or Godot APIs. Avoid `:=` in `run_scene_edit_script` unless the type is completely obvious and diagnostics-safe. Script diagnostics can treat Variant-based inference warnings as blocking errors, so typed code is the default style for this tool. Good examples: `var root: Node = ctx.get_scene_root()`, `var speed: float = 8.0`, `var count: int = 0`, `var enabled: bool = true`, `var panel: Variant = root.get("theme_override_styles/panel")`, `var children: Array[Node] = root.get_children()`, `var sb: StyleBox = c.get_theme_stylebox(slot)`.

Safe default strategy: start with a tiny probe before a broad rewrite. First log the root class/name. Then read one exact known property. Then mutate one exact resource. Only after that should you expand into scene-wide traversal or bulk edits.

Stored-property access vs live-theme access are different. For known targets, prefer direct property access first, e.g. `var panel = root.get("theme_override_styles/panel")`. Use live theme helpers only when you intentionally want theme lookup behavior, e.g. `control.get_theme_stylebox("panel")`.

Godot API names must not be guessed. Before using methods on a native Godot class, inspect that class with `get_class_info` and use the real documented API. If you call a non-existent method, treat that first as a likely wrong API name on the native class.

AnimationPlayer library rule: in Godot 4.x, do not probe a missing default animation library with `player.get_animation_library("")`; it can log an engine error before returning null. Use `player.has_animation_library("")` first, or create a fresh `AnimationLibrary` and add it with `player.add_animation_library("", library)` before adding animations.

AnimationTree authoring rule: creating an `AnimationTree` or state machine in the saved scene only makes it editor-visible. Runtime scripts still need to drive `AnimationNodeStateMachinePlayback` explicitly if gameplay should use the tree; otherwise direct `AnimationPlayer.play(...)` helpers remain the active runtime path.

Placeholder-instance error interpretation: if Godot says `Attempt to call a method on a placeholder instance. Check if the script is in tool mode.`, it usually means the target node has an attached script and is loaded in editor context as a placeholder-style scripted instance rather than a fully live gameplay/runtime script instance.

What that DOES mean: the node is being seen through editor-time/script-placeholder semantics, so you should be cautious about inferring full runtime behavior from it, especially for attached-script behavior.
What that does NOT mean: it does NOT automatically prove that built-in methods on the node's native Godot class are unusable, and it does NOT automatically explain the failure by itself.

General rule: if a node has an attached script and Godot reports a placeholder-instance error during a method call, do not immediately conclude that placeholder mode blocked all native API access. First verify whether the method itself is valid on the node's native class.

Interpretation priority: (1) confirm the node's real native class, (2) confirm the method exists on that native class via `get_class_info`, (3) only then reason about placeholder/editor-mode limitations. Do not use placeholder wording as an excuse to skip native API verification.

Concrete proven example: on scripted `Control` roots loaded by this tool, valid built-in calls like `get_theme_stylebox(...)` still worked, while the non-existent `get_theme_stylebox_override(...)` failed. The right lesson is general: a placeholder-instance message can coexist with a simple wrong-API call.

Logging is part of the implementation, not an afterthought. For any nontrivial script, log root class, root name, target node, target slot/property, before value, after value, and final summary counts. Detailed logs are often the difference between quickly fixing a script and misdiagnosing the tool.

Editor-state rule: screenshot and validation passes can reveal saved-editor state, not just runtime behavior. Hide modals, toasts, debug labels, enemy health overlays, and other runtime-only UI before saving generated scenes unless the user explicitly wants them visible in editor screenshots.

One-off builder script storage rule: temporary scene builders and scene edit worker scripts must live only in Fennara-managed scratch space under `res://.fennara/`, normally `res://.fennara/tmp/editor_scripts/`. Do NOT save these one-off scripts in user project folders such as `res://scripts/`, `res://scripts/editor_builders/`, `res://addons/`, or alongside gameplay code. Do not attach them as runtime gameplay scripts.

Be careful: some debug/logging APIs can fail the run. In particular, instantiated nodes from this tool are often NOT inside a live SceneTree, so `node.get_path()` can emit runtime errors and fail the tool run. Prefer `get_name()`, `get_parent()`, `get_children()`, or build debug paths manually by walking parents.

Related safety rule: treat `get_tree()`, `get_path()`, `is_inside_tree()` assumptions, timers, and frame-processing logic as risky unless you have explicitly proven they work for your case. Object-graph/property access is usually safer than SceneTree-global/runtime behavior.

Node3D orientation gotcha: nodes added with `parent.add_child(node)` are part of the scene object graph being saved, but they may still not behave like nodes inside a live running SceneTree. Avoid `node.look_at(...)` on newly created cameras, markers, lights, or Node3D props in edit scripts; Godot may report that the node is not inside the tree. Prefer `node.look_at_from_position(node.position, target_position, Vector3.UP)` when orienting from a known position, or assign `rotation`/`basis` directly for authored local scene geometry.
Editor-safe camera example: `camera.position = Vector3(0.0, 3.0, -8.0); camera.look_at_from_position(camera.position, Vector3(0.0, 1.0, 0.0), Vector3.UP)`.

The response includes `script_diagnostics`, `runtime_errors`, `logs`, `scene_created`, `scene_saved`, `modified`, `scene_status_note`, `inherited_root_scene` when relevant, `inherited_root_preserved` when an inherited root was successfully preserved, and the effective `script_path`. If diagnostics report errors, execution stops before running the script — patch that same path with `write_or_update_file`; do not rewrite the whole script from scratch and resend it as `code`.

Important result contract: a failed `run_scene_edit_script` does NOT create or update the target scene. Only the temp script file may persist for patch-and-rerun flows. Do not assume the scene exists or was saved just because `scene_path` is returned in a failure result.

scene_path.description: Target scene file to modify or create, e.g. `res://generated/fps/two_story_building.tscn`. This is the only scene the script is meant to touch.

code.description: Raw GDScript for an initial save+run. If you already have a `script_path` from a previous attempt (especially after errors), prefer **`write_or_update_file` + `script_path`** instead of pasting the whole script again. Provide exactly one of `code` or `script_path`.

script_path.description: Existing `.gd` to run (required on retries). After any failure or partial run, edit the file at this path with **`write_or_update_file`** then pass the same path here — avoid sending a full new `code` string for large scripts. Provide exactly one of `code` or `script_path`.
